frame: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment:
Meena: "Suno ji, ek baat gaur kari kya aapne? Ritu... shaadi mein toh kitne achhe se sindoor lagaya tha. Par ab ghar aane ke baad se, zyada din toh sirf mangalsutra hi pehnti hai, sindoor nahi lagati."
Meena: "Ek nayi bahu hai, log kya kahenge? Yeh toh suhagan ki nishani hoti hai na. Humare Khandaan mein toh aise hi chalta hai. Acha nahi lagta."
Meena: "Mujhe lagta hai Karan ko hi thoda samjhana chahiye. Ab hum seedhe kya kahenge usko? Par yeh zaroori hai."
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment is misogynistic as it subtly enforces traditional gendered expectations on Ritu's appearance (sindoor) as an indicator of her marital status and proper conduct as a 'new bahu'. Meena leverages societal judgment ('log kya kahenge') and family tradition ('Humare Khandaan mein toh aise hi chalta hai') to pressure Ritu into conforming, suggesting Karan should intervene indirectly. This encodes the surface manifestation of 'Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha Pola, Name Change: Looking Married'.
persona_text: You are Meena. Age 54. Married for 28 years. A homemaker in Jaipur. Your son Karan who is 29 just got married to a girl named Ritu who is from Delhi. Ritu is a working professional and seems confident and modern. At the wedding Ritu wore sindoor and the mangalsutra but since coming to your house she has only worn the mangalsutra Ñ no sindoor on most days. You have not said anything to Ritu. But you are sitting with your husband at night after Ritu and Karan have gone to their room. You are telling your husband what you have observed and what it means and what you think needs to be said and by whom.
